draft.title: weather app for runners
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
weather app for runners

Building your first draft…

facade.hypothesis: A weather app for runners benefits from a friendly, encouraging voice—brief check-ins and motivational language—rather than neutral, data-focused language.
facade.label: Conversational tone
facade.acceptImplies: Users want personality and warmth in their running companion; design should feel supportive and human-centered.
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer clean, factual information without personality overlay; voice should stay neutral and get out of the way.

facade.hypothesis: High-contrast, digitally-bright interface with electric color accents creates energetic, performance-focused mood — appeals to runners who want intensity and visibility
facade.label: Vibrant neon text
facade.acceptImplies: User gravitates toward bold, tech-forward aesthetic; may prefer glanceable data displays, high saturation, modern typefaces
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers softer, more natural or muted palette; likely favors clarity through restraint rather than contrast; may want earthy or monochrome tones

evidence.hypothesis: A weather app for runners benefits from a friendly, encouraging voice—brief check-ins and motivational language—rather than neutral, data-focused language.
evidence.implication: Users want personality and warmth in their running companion; design should feel supportive and human-centered.

facade.hypothesis: Runners respond energetically to vibrant, dynamic color transitions in sports apps—suggests active, motivational aesthetic over muted or neutral palettes
facade.label: Bold colorful gradient
facade.acceptImplies: Design leans toward bright, multi-hued, high-energy visuals; weather data displayed with saturated accent colors; possibly neon or sunset-inspired gradients
facade.rejectImplies: Design favors restraint in color; weather info in muted, desaturated, or monochromatic tones; cooler or more minimalist palette is preferred for runner focus

facade.hypothesis: For a runner app, immediate feedback on taps (no loading states or transition delay) feels more responsive and trustworthy than animated transitions between screens.
facade.axisTargeted: Instant vs animated transitions — whether UI responds with zero-latency clicks or fluid motion between states.
facade.label: Tap updates instantly
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers snappy, direct feedback; likely values speed and certainty over visual smoothness; may want minimal animations overall.
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers motion and continuity; likely wants to see where content comes from and goes; may accept slight delay if it feels intentional and connected.

facade.hypothesis: Runners want granular pace/distance data displayed as real-time segments (mile markers, kilometer splits) rather than aggregate summary stats
facade.axisTargeted: DENSE DATA vs KEY METRICS — should the app show detailed breakdowns or just the essential headline numbers?
facade.label: Splits and intervals
facade.acceptImplies: The design favors data-rich, segment-based readouts; runners scan detailed splits mid-run; the interface prioritizes precision and breakdown over simplicity
facade.rejectImplies: The design favors headline metrics (total distance, average pace, time); runners want glanceable summaries; the interface prioritizes clarity and speed-of-understanding over granular detail

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
Whether user wants quick-glance widgets vs. deep-dive dashboard navigation for weather checks mid-run
Whether user responds to motivational, encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual weather reporting
Whether user wants energizing, action-oriented feel vs. calm, meditative interface atmosphere
Whether user prioritizes simple essentials (temp, rain) vs. detailed metrics (humidity, wind, UV index)
axes.poleA: Just the basics or all the stats

axes.label: Whether user prefers smooth, flowing transitions vs. snappy, instant feedback and alerts

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants quick-glance widgets vs. deep-dive dashboard navigation for weather checks mid-run
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to motivational, encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual weather reporting
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants energizing, action-oriented feel vs. calm, meditative interface atmosphere
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prioritizes simple essentials (temp, rain) vs. detailed metrics (humidity, wind, UV index)
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers smooth, flowing transitions vs. snappy, instant feedback and alerts
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: A persistent left sidebar navigation (with running metrics like pace, distance, heart rate) paired with vertical card stacks for weather details (current, hourly, alerts) creates faster glanceability than a full-screen tab layout.
facade.axisTargeted: Navigation structure: sidebar persistence vs. tab-based switching
facade.label: Sidebar with cards
facade.acceptImplies: Users value always-visible metrics + modular weather cards. Explore sidebar depth (collapsed vs. expanded), card height (tall vs. shallow), and metric priority ordering.
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer immersive full-screen views or tab-based compartmentalization. Probe single-screen density and swipeable card carousels instead.

facade.hypothesis: A weather app for runners benefits from colloquial, runner-specific language (e.g., 'conditions are gnarly,' 'perfect for a tempo run') rather than formal meteorological terminology, creating in-group belonging and real-world relevance.
facade.axisTargeted: Voice formality and domain specificity (neutral technical vs. community insider)
facade.label: Casual slang
facade.acceptImplies: Design voice as an experienced running buddy who speaks your language; use runner lexicon and casual phrasing throughout the app.
facade.rejectImplies: Stick closer to standard weather app language; avoid jargon that might confuse or alienate non-hardcore runners; prioritize clarity over community flavor.

draft.title: RunWeather
draft.summary: A weather app designed for runners, showing real-time conditions, wind impact, and run-friendly forecasts at a glance.
